items.question: I ___ smoke, but I quit years ago.
explanations: Correct! A finished past habit takes 'used to': 'I used to smoke'.
'Use to' (no -d) is only for negatives and questions after 'did'.
'Am used to' means 'accustomed to', a different meaning.
'Would have' is a conditional form, not a past habit here.

items.question: There ___ a castle on that hill.
explanations: 'Would' can't describe a past state like 'be'.
Correct! For a past state, use 'used to': 'There used to be a castle'.
'Uses to be' isn't correct — the past form is 'used to be'.
'Would have' is a different structure.

items.question: I ___ like vegetables when I was young.
explanations: After 'didn't' you drop the -d: 'use to', not 'used to'.
Correct! The negative is 'didn't use to like'.
'Don't' is present tense; this is about the past.
'Wasn't used to' means 'wasn't accustomed to', a different meaning.

items.question: Every winter, we ___ go skiing in the Pyrenees.
explanations: Correct! A repeated past action (a story) can take 'would': 'we would go skiing'.
'Are used to' means 'accustomed to', a different meaning.
'Use to' (no -d) is only for negatives and questions after 'did'.
'Would have' is a conditional form, not a past habit.

items.question: ___ you ___ live in Madrid?
explanations: Correct! Questions use 'Did + subject + use to': 'Did you use to live…?'.
After 'did' drop the -d: 'use to', not 'used to'.
'Do you use to' is present; this is about the past.
'Are you used to' means 'accustomed to', a different meaning.

items.question: I ___ getting up early now — it feels normal.
explanations: 'Used to + base verb' is a finished past habit, but here you mean 'accustomed'.
Correct! 'Be used to + -ing' means accustomed: 'I am used to getting up early'.
'Would' describes past actions, not being accustomed now.
'Use to' isn't correct here; use 'am used to'.

items.question: Correct this: 'My grandmother would have a big garden.'
options: My grandmother used to have a big garden.
My grandmother would has a big garden.
My grandmother use to have a big garden.
My grandmother is used to have a big garden.
explanations: Correct! 'Have a garden' is a past state, so use 'used to have'.
'Would has' is grammatically wrong.
'Use to' (no -d) is only for negatives/questions after 'did'.
'Is used to have' mixes structures and changes the meaning.

items.question: We ___ play in the street as kids.
explanations: Correct! A repeated past habit takes 'used to' (or 'would'): 'We used to play'.
'Are used to' means 'accustomed to', a different meaning.
'Use to' (no -d) is only for negatives/questions after 'did'.
'Would have' is a conditional form.

items.question: Rewrite with the same meaning: 'In the past I played the piano, but not anymore.'
options: I used to play the piano.
I would have played the piano.
I am used to playing the piano.
I use to play the piano.
explanations: Correct! A finished past habit takes 'used to': 'I used to play the piano'.
'Would have played' is conditional, not a past habit.
'Am used to playing' means accustomed now, not a finished past habit.
'Use to' (no -d) is only for negatives/questions after 'did'.

items.question: He ___ cycle to work, but now he drives.
explanations: Correct! A finished past habit takes 'used to': 'He used to cycle to work'.
'Is used to' means accustomed now, but he doesn't cycle any more.
'Use to' (no -d) is only for negatives/questions after 'did'.
'Would have' is a conditional form.
